
# Project Plan

## Phase 0: Start-up (June 2026, Weeks 1–4)

- Establish collaboration infrastructure: GitHub issue tracking, pull request templates, contributor guidelines.
- Review existing `partitions` codebase: C API, R wrapper functions, test coverage, and GitHub open issues.
- Draft S3 `partitions_iter` class structure and method signatures.
- Set up continuous integration (CI) via GitHub Actions to ensure all changes pass `R CMD check` and `testthat` suite.
- Finalise scope and technical design with Robin Hankin.

**Effort:** 8 hours (Sam Kamperis), 8 hours (Robin Hankin)

---

## Phase 1: Core Implementation (July 2026, Weeks 5–8)

- Implement `iparts()`, `irestrictedparts()`, `idiffparts()` factory functions.
- Implement `nextElem.partitions_iter()` method for each enumeration type.
- Ensure backward compatibility: existing `parts()`, `firstpart()`, `nextpart()` APIs remain unchanged.
- Write unit tests (`testthat`) covering basic iteration, edge cases (n=1, n=0, m>n, etc.), and concurrent iterator coexistence.
- Robin Hankin: technical guidance and review (4 hours).

**Effort:** 16 hours (Sam Kamperis), 8 hours (Robin Hankin)

---

## Phase 2: Integration & Testing (August 2026, Weeks 9–12)

- Integrate with `foreach` package: test that `foreach(part = iparts(n)) %do%` and `%dopar%` work correctly.
- Integrate with `itertools` package: verify chaining via `itertools::ichain()`, filtering via `ifilter()`, etc.
- Address outstanding GitHub issues (bug fixes, documentation improvements) in the context of the iterator framework.
- Expand `testthat` suite to cover parallel iteration, GitHub issue resolution, and mathematical correctness benchmarks.

**Effort:** 16 hours (Sam Kamperis), 4 hours (Robin Hankin)

---

## Phase 3: Vignette & Documentation (September 2026, Weeks 13–18)

- Write a detailed Quarto vignette (5+ worked examples):
  1. Serial enumeration: Basic `while` loop with `nextElem()`.
  2. `foreach()` parallelisation: Distributing partition enumeration across cores/cluster.
  3. `itertools` composition: Chaining, filtering, zipping partition iterators.
  4. Memory-efficiency comparison: Bulk generation (`parts(n)`) vs. lazy iteration (`iparts(n)`) for large n.
  5. Real-world use case: Sampling partitions, computing partition statistics, parallel optimisation.
- Update package `README.md` with modern iterator examples.
- Ensure all R documentation (`.Rd` files) reflects new iterator functions.
- Add CRAN-compliant licensing headers if necessary.
- Robin Hankin: two-day focused visit in Oxford (8 hours).

**Effort:** 16 hours (Sam Kamperis), 16 hours (Robin Hankin)

---

## Phase 4: Review & Release (October 2026, Weeks 19–22)

- Code review with Robin Hankin; incorporate feedback.
- Final `R CMD check` and comprehensive testing.
- Prepare CRAN submission: ensure all checks pass, update `NEWS.md`, verify compatibility.
- Integrate 5-hour contingency for extended review, issue triage, or unexpected fixes.
- Robin Hankin: technical guidance and review (4 hours).

**Effort:** 16 hours (Sam Kamperis), 4 hours (Robin Hankin)

---

## Phase 5: Publicity & Dissemination (November 2026, Weeks 23–26)

- Submit to CRAN.
- Address CRAN feedback (if any) and release new package version.
- Announce release on R Consortium blog and community channels.
- Robin Hankin: technical guidance and review (4 hours).

**Effort:** 8 hours (Sam Kamperis), 4 hours (Robin Hankin)

---

## Budget & Funding Plan
**Total Funding:** $18,331.21 
**Duration:** 6 months (1 June – 30 November 2026)  
**Total Hours:** 124 (Sam Kamperis: 80, Robin Hankin: 44)


| Phase | Dates | Sam Kamperis (hrs) | Robin Hankin (hrs) | Deliverables | Funding (%) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Phase 0: Start-up** | June | 8 | 8 | Collaboration infra, design doc, CI setup | 12% |
| **Phase 1: Core Implementation** | July | 16 | 8 | Iterator factory functions, unit tests, technical review | 16% |
| **Phase 2: Integration & Testing** | August | 16 | 4 | `foreach` & `itertools` integration, expanded tests | 16% |
| **Phase 3: Vignette & Documentation** | Sept | 16 | 16 | Comprehensive Quarto vignette, updated docs, Oxford visit | 37% |
| **Phase 4: Review & Release** | Oct | 16 | 4 | CRAN submission, code review, contingency | 17% |
| **Phase 5: Publicity & Dissemination** | Nov | 8 | 4 | Public announcement, CRAN feedback | 8% |
| **Total** | June–Nov | **80** | **44** | Full modernisation + CRAN release | **100%** |

## Technical Delivery

<!--
Covering the actual delivery of the project this section should
provide at least a high-level description of the implementation.
Including target dates is really important as you need to be committed
and the ISC need to have a means of tracking delivery
-->

### Phase 0: Start-up (June 2026, Weeks 1–4)

- Establish collaboration infrastructure: GitHub issue tracking, pull request templates, contributor guidelines.
- Review existing `partitions` codebase: C API, R wrapper functions, test coverage, and GitHub open issues.
- Draft S3 `partitions_iter` class structure and method signatures.
- Set up continuous integration (CI) via GitHub Actions to ensure all changes pass `R CMD check` and `testthat` suite.
- Finalise scope and technical design with Robin Hankin.

**Effort:** 8 hours (Sam Kamperis), 8 hours (Robin Hankin)

---

### Phase 1: Core Implementation (July 2026, Weeks 5–8)

- Implement `iparts()`, `irestrictedparts()`, `idiffparts()` factory functions.
- Implement `nextElem.partitions_iter()` method for each enumeration type.
- Ensure backward compatibility: existing `parts()`, `firstpart()`, `nextpart()` APIs remain unchanged.
- Write unit tests (`testthat`) covering basic iteration, edge cases (n=1, n=0, m>n, etc.), and concurrent iterator coexistence.
- Robin Hankin: technical guidance and review (8 hours).

**Effort:** 16 hours (Sam Kamperis), 8 hours (Robin Hankin)

---

### Phase 2: Integration & Testing (August 2026, Weeks 9–12)

- Integrate with `foreach` package: test that `foreach(part = iparts(n)) %do%` and `%dopar%` work correctly.
- Integrate with `itertools` package: verify chaining via `itertools::ichain()`, filtering via `ifilter()`, etc.
- Address outstanding GitHub issues (bug fixes, documentation improvements) in the context of the iterator framework.
- Expand `testthat` suite to cover parallel iteration, GitHub issue resolution, and mathematical correctness benchmarks.
- Robin Hankin: technical guidance and review (4 hours).

**Effort:** 16 hours (Sam Kamperis), 4 hours (Robin Hankin)

---

### Phase 3: Vignette & Documentation (September 2026, Weeks 13–18)

- Write a detailed Quarto vignette (5+ worked examples):
  1. Serial enumeration: Basic `while` loop with `nextElem()`.
  2. `foreach()` parallelisation: Distributing partition enumeration across cores/cluster.
  3. `itertools` composition: Chaining, filtering, zipping partition iterators.
  4. Memory-efficiency comparison: Bulk generation (`parts(n)`) vs. lazy iteration (`iparts(n)`) for large n.
  5. Real-world use case: Sampling partitions, computing partition statistics, parallel optimisation.
- Update package `README.md` with modern iterator examples.
- Ensure all R documentation (`.Rd` files) reflects new iterator functions.
- Add CRAN-compliant licensing headers if necessary.
- Robin Hankin: two-day focused visit in Oxford (16 hours).

**Effort:** 16 hours (Sam Kamperis), 16 hours (Robin Hankin)

---

### Phase 4: Review & Release (October 2026, Weeks 19–22)

- Code review with Robin Hankin; incorporate feedback.
- Final `R CMD check` and comprehensive testing.
- Prepare CRAN submission: ensure all checks pass, update `NEWS.md`, verify compatibility.
- Integrate 5-hour contingency for extended review, issue triage, or unexpected fixes.
- Robin Hankin: technical guidance and review (4 hours).

**Effort:** 16 hours (Sam Kamperis), 4 hours (Robin Hankin)

---

### Phase 5: Publicity & Dissemination (November 2026, Weeks 23–26)

- Submit to CRAN.
- Address CRAN feedback (if any) and release new package version.
- Announce release on R Consortium blog and community channels.
- Robin Hankin: technical guidance and review (4 hours).

**Effort:** 8 hours (Sam Kamperis), 4 hours (Robin Hankin)



---

## Other Aspects

<!--
How will you ensure that your work is available to the widest number
of people? Please specify the open-source or creative commons license(s)
you will use, how you will host your code so that others can contribute,
and how you will publicize your work. We encourage you to plan content
to be shared quarterly on the R Consortium blog.

 - [ ] Announcement post
 - [ ] Delivery blog post
 - [ ] Social media
 - [ ] UseR!
 - [ ] ISC meetings
-->

### Availability & Licensing

The `partitions` package is licensed under GPL-3. All code, tests, and documentation produced by this project will be released under the same licence, ensuring consistency and compatibility.

Code will be hosted on the official GitHub repository ([github.com/RobinHankin/partitions](https://github.com/RobinHankin/partitions)) with pull requests and issues tracked transparently.

### Publicity & Dissemination

- **ISC Blog Post (Mid-project, ~July 2026):** Summary of modernisation goals and progress.
- **Delivery Blog Post (Post-release, ~December 2026):** Technical introduction to the new iterator API, with code examples and benchmarks.
- **R Community Channels:** Announcements on R Weekly, R Discourse, and relevant social media.
- **Conference Consideration:** Abstract submission to useR! 2027 if timeline permits.

## Budget & Funding Plan

<!--
Outline the milestones for development and how much funding will be
required for each stage (as payments will be tied to project milestone
completion). Each milestone should specify the work to be done and the
expected outcomes, providing enough detail for the ISC to understand
the scope of the project work and assess the likelihood of success.

We expect that most of the budget will be allocated
for labor costs. We do not cover indirect costs. The ISC grants cannot
cover such things as travel, lodging, food, journal publication fees,
or personal hardware. Cloud services may be covered if they are specific
to the project and the project period. The ISC reserves the right to vet
how funds are used for each project separately. If in doubt, please reach
out to us.
-->


### Budget Allocation
**Total Funding:** $18,331.21
**Duration:** 6 months (1 June – 30 November 2026)  
**Total Hours:** 124 (Sam Kamperis: 80, Robin Hankin: 44)

| Phase | Dates | Sam Kamperis (hrs) | Robin Hankin (hrs) | Deliverables | Funding (%) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Phase 0: Start-up** | June | 8 | 8 | Collaboration infra, design doc, CI setup | 12% |
| **Phase 1: Core Implementation** | July | 16 | 8 | Iterator factory functions, unit tests, technical review | 16% |
| **Phase 2: Integration & Testing** | August | 16 | 4 | `foreach` & `itertools` integration, expanded tests | 16% |
| **Phase 3: Vignette & Documentation** | Sept | 16 | 16 | Comprehensive Quarto vignette, updated docs, Oxford visit | 37% |
| **Phase 4: Review & Release** | Oct | 16 | 4 | CRAN submission, code review, contingency | 17% |
| **Phase 5: Publicity & Dissemination** | Nov | 8 | 4 | Public announcement, CRAN feedback | 8% |
| **Total** | June–Nov | **80** | **44** | Full modernisation + CRAN release | **100%** |

**Payment Schedule:** 50% of the grant is paid upon contract signing, and 50% upon completion. The contingency (5 hours) is included in Phase 4 for extended review or unexpected issues.
